morning_brief.title: Morning Brief
morning_brief.description:
Goal: Generate a daily Morning Brief by fetching GitHub, Google Calendar, and Gmail in parallel, then:

  • Send the brief to Slack.
  • Update today's scratchpad with the relevant action items and context.

Execution Order (must follow)

  1. Fetch in parallel: GitHub + Calendar + Gmail.
  2. Formulate one consolidated brief (single output).
  3. Send the brief over Slack (human-readable summary).
  4. Update scratchpad (structured, durable record; action-oriented).

1) GitHub

Scope: Last 24 hours and anything currently waiting on Harshith.

  • PRs awaiting your review.
  • PRs you opened that have review comments or CI changes since last check.
  • Issues/PRs where you were mentioned/assigned in the last 24 hours.
  • CI failures or "red" statuses on PRs you care about.

Output expectations: For each item, include repo/name, what changed, and the next action (if any).

2) Calendar

Scope: Today's events only (user-local timezone).

  • List meetings with time and title.
  • Flag external meetings (attendees outside your org domain) as "External".
  • Call out anything that looks like it needs preparation.

3) Gmail

Scope: Emails received yesterday and today only (resolve actual dates at runtime).

Read the subject line of each email in scope and classify it into one of 4 categories:

Categories:

  • Action Required — Emails from people or organisations that need your response or attention. This includes your internal team, key vendors, and customers of your product or business. These are emails where the ball is in your court.
  • FYI — Informational only, no action needed. Examples: payment confirmations, bank notifications, product updates, calendar invites.
  • Newsletters — Subscription emails, digests, editorial content.
  • Spam — Unsolicited sales or promotional outreach.

Setup — run before classifying emails:

Search memory for the following before asking the user anything:

  • "user's internal team email domain or company domain"
  • "user's key vendors, suppliers, or service providers"
  • "user's product name or how their customers are identified"
  • "email classification rules or inbox priorities"

Use whatever is found to pre-populate the classification logic. Only ask about the pieces that are still missing.

If nothing is found, ask the user once:

"To classify your emails accurately, I need a few details:

  • What is your internal team's email domain? (e.g. @yourcompany.com)
  • Who are your key vendors or service providers I should watch for?
  • How do I identify emails from your customers or product users?

You can describe them by email address, domain, or company name."

Store all answers in memory. Never ask again once stored.

Processing rules by category:

  • Action Required: Read full body. Output: Full summary + recommended action (reply needed? Draft a response?)
  • FYI: Read full body. Output: 1-2 line summary.
  • Newsletters: Subject line only.
  • Spam: Read full body. Output: One-liner on what they're selling.

4) Slack Delivery (send message)

Goal: Deliver a concise, high-signal Morning Brief message over Slack.

Format: Use short sections (GitHub / Calendar / Email), keep it scannable, and end with a short "Top actions" list.

5) Scratchpad Update (keep this structure EXACTLY)

Runs daily (default 9am user-local). Goal: when the user opens their scratchpad in the morning, the top of the page shows what's already on their plate plus anything the system noticed in the last 24h.

  1. Read today's scratchpad first. Call get_scratchpad with no date (defaults to today). If a "Brief —" heading already exists for today, STOP — the brief has already been written. Do not write a duplicate.
  2. Gather data. In parallel where possible:
    • Carried over: still-Todo tasks (list_tasks status=Todo) — include open count days from createdAt.
    • Suggested today: from connected integrations (use relevant integration tools to peek at the last 24h — unread important emails, today's calendar, PRs awaiting your review, @mentions you haven't responded to).
    • Heads up: non-actionable but worth knowing — meeting moves, deploys, ambient changes from integrations.
  3. Compose the brief as plain HTML using EXACTLY this structure. Omit any section that has zero items. Cap each section at 5 entries.
    <h3>Brief — {Weekday, Month Day}</h3>

<h4>Carried over</h4>
<ul>
  <li>{task title} <em>(open {N} days)</em></li>
</ul>

<h4>Suggested today</h4>
<ul>
  <li>{verb-first action item, ~8 words}</li>
</ul>

<h4>Heads up</h4>
<ul>
  <li>{short factual note}</li>
</ul>
  4. Write to scratchpad. Call update_scratchpad with the HTML above, mode="append", date omitted. This adds the brief to today's page without disturbing whatever the user has already written.
  5. No channel notification. Do NOT call send_message. The brief is meant to be discovered when the user opens their scratchpad — pinging them defeats the purpose.

Rules

  • Plain bullets, not <taskItem> nodes. The user converts items to real tasks manually if they want. Suggestions should not auto-create tasks.
  • Dedupe. Items in "Carried over" must not also appear in "Suggested today".
  • Be conservative. Better to surface 3 high-quality items than 5 marginal ones. An empty section is fine.
  • If everything is empty, still write the minimal heading (<h3>Brief — ...</h3>) so the user can see the brief ran. No body required.
  • Never replace. Always mode="append". The user's own scratchpad writing is sacred.

When to skip entirely

  • If today's scratchpad already has a "Brief —" heading (step 1).
  • If the user has explicitly turned the morning brief task off (handled by the scheduled task being inactive).

inbox_midday_catchup.title: Inbox Mid-day Catch-up
inbox_midday_catchup.description:
Goal: Second-pass triage on email that arrived since the Morning Brief, so the user doesn't have to context-switch into their inbox between meetings.

Scope

Email received since 9am today (resolve actual time at runtime). Skip anything already covered in this morning's brief.

Triage rules

Apply the user's "What's Important in My Inbox" policy to classify each email:

  • Action Required — full summary + suggested reply.
  • FYI — one-line summary.
  • Skip silently — newsletters, automated notifications, marketing.

Delivery

Post a short Slack message — only the Action Required and FYI sections. If both are empty, skip the message entirely (no "nothing important" pings).

Format

Mid-day catch-up — {time}

**Action required** ({N})
- {sender}: {one-line summary} → {suggested action}

**FYI** ({N})
- {sender}: {one-line summary}

Rules

  • Don't re-surface items the Morning Brief already covered — check the scratchpad's "Brief —" heading for today and dedupe.
  • Cap each section at 5 entries.
  • No scratchpad write. This task is push-only; the morning brief owns the scratchpad.

end_of_day_wrapup.title: End-of-Day Wrap-up
end_of_day_wrapup.description:
Goal: Close the loop on today before the user signs off. Surface what got done, what's still open, and what's blocking — so tomorrow's Morning Brief can pick up from a clean state.

Sources (fetch in parallel)

  • GitHub — PRs merged today, PRs reviewed today, commits pushed.
  • Tasks — tasks moved to Done today, tasks still in Working or Waiting.
  • Email — threads where the user replied today, threads still waiting on a reply (apply the "What's Important in My Inbox" policy).
  • Calendar — meetings attended today (context only — not part of the output).

Output

Post a single Slack message with three sections:

  • Shipped today — bullets for completed work (PRs merged, tasks Done, important replies sent). Cap at 7 entries.
  • Still open — bullets for tasks in Working / Waiting that haven't moved. Include how long they've been open. Cap at 5.
  • Blocking — anything where the user is the bottleneck (PRs requesting review they haven't given, threads waiting on a reply > 24h). Cap at 3.

Rules

  • Skip empty sections. If nothing shipped, don't pad — just say so.
  • Don't repeat items from this morning's brief unless their status changed today.
  • If all three sections are empty, send a one-line "Quiet day." and stop.
  • No scratchpad write — this is push-only to Slack.

weekly_engineering_report.title: Weekly Engineering Report
weekly_engineering_report.description: Every Friday at 5pm, pull the last 7 days of GitHub metrics for the user's repos and apply their engineering-analytics lens. Highlight only what moved or crossed a healthy threshold — cap observations at 3-5 bullets. Log the table to the user's Google Sheets engineering tracker.

weekly_retrospective.title: Weekly Retrospective Prompt
weekly_retrospective.description:
Goal: Drop a structured journaling prompt into the user's scratchpad every Friday afternoon. Habit-building for weekly reflection. The user fills it in themselves — this task only writes the scaffold.

What to write

Append the following HTML to today's scratchpad using update_scratchpad with mode="append". Date omitted.

<h3>Retro — Week of {Monday, Month Day} – {Friday, Month Day}</h3>

<p><strong>What worked?</strong></p>
<ul><li></li></ul>

<p><strong>What didn't?</strong></p>
<ul><li></li></ul>

<p><strong>What to change next week?</strong></p>
<ul><li></li></ul>

Rules

  • Empty bullets (single <li></li>) — the user fills them in. Don't pre-populate with guesses.
  • If the scratchpad already has a "Retro —" heading for this week, skip silently.
  • No Slack message. The retro is a private journaling exercise.
  • The Sunday Planning task reads the most recent retro's "What to change next week?" — keep this structure stable so that link doesn't break.

sunday_planning.title: Sunday Planning
sunday_planning.description:
Goal: Draft the week ahead on Sunday evening — calendar review, unscheduled high-priority work surfaced, deep-work blocks suggested. Posted to scratchpad for the user to refine before Monday.

Sources (fetch in parallel)

  • Calendar — Monday through Friday of next week, all events.
  • Tasks — open tasks across all statuses, sorted by priority then age. Old open tasks bubble up.
  • Last week's retro — search scratchpad for the most recent "Retro —" heading. If "What to change next week?" has user content, surface it at the top.

Output

Append to today's scratchpad (mode="append"):

<h3>Week ahead — {Monday Month Day} to {Friday Month Day}</h3>

<h4>Carried from last retro</h4>
<ul><li>{what to change next week, verbatim from the most recent retro}</li></ul>

<h4>Meeting load</h4>
<p>{N meetings, M hours total}. Heaviest day: {weekday}.</p>

<h4>Top open work</h4>
<ul>
  <li>{task title} <em>(open {N} days)</em></li>
</ul>

<h4>Suggested deep-work blocks</h4>
<ul>
  <li>{weekday} {start}–{end}: {≥2 hour gap with no meetings}</li>
</ul>

Rules

  • Omit sections with no content (e.g. no retro this week → skip Carried-over).
  • Cap Top open work at 7 entries. Pick highest priority, oldest first as tie-breaker.
  • Deep-work blocks: identify ≥2 hour contiguous gaps in next week's calendar. Surface up to 5.
  • No Slack — discovery model, not push. The user finds it when they open Monday morning.
  • Never replace. Always mode="append".

weekly_customer_digest.title: Weekly Customer Feedback Digest
weekly_customer_digest.description:
Goal: Pull last week's customer signals from email and group them by theme. Catches patterns that get lost in daily noise.

Scope

Email received in the last 7 days that the user's "What's Important in My Inbox" policy classifies as a customer signal:

  • Bug reports
  • Feature requests
  • Support questions
  • Feedback (positive or negative)
  • Cancellation / churn signals

Processing

  1. Pull all customer-signal emails from the last 7 days.
  2. Group by theme — bugs together, feature requests together, etc.
  3. Within each group, identify patterns: the same bug from multiple users, the same feature requested 3+ times.
  4. Flag urgent items: cancellations, recurring bug reports, frustrated tone.

Output

Post to Slack with sections:

  • Urgent — churn signals, recurring critical bugs. If empty, skip the section.
  • Bugs — grouped by bug, with count of affected users.
  • Feature requests — grouped by ask, with count. Lead with patterns (3+ asks).
  • Sentiment — one-paragraph summary of overall tone this week. Use exact quotes where useful.

Rules

  • If a single customer sent 5 emails about the same issue, count once.
  • Include sender's email/name when surfacing a quote — the user may want to reply.
  • Cap each section at 7 entries. If more, summarise the tail in one line.
  • If no customer signals this week, send a one-line "No customer signals this week." and stop.

research_account.title: Research an Account
research_account.description: Take a company or person name from the user and apply their account-research lens. Lead with CORE memory, then web search, then enrichment/CRM if connected. Output the report in the structure the user prefers — quick take, memory context, company snapshot, key people, best angle, sources.

money_subscription_identifier.title: Weekly Subscription Identifier
money_subscription_identifier.description:
Goal: Maintain a living ledger of every subscription I have — what's active, what's been cancelled, and the delta this week. The output is a persistent two-section view (Subscriptions + Cancelled subscriptions) that grows over time, not just a weekly delta.

Applies the lens: "How I Categorize My Money" — Section 1 (email types), Section 3 (payee mapping), Section 4 (subscription detection and stored fields).

Two run modes

  • Bootstrap (first run, or memory has no subscription state) — scan the last 3 months (90 days) of Gmail. Build the initial Subscriptions list and start an empty Cancelled subscriptions list. 3 months covers a 3-occurrence confirmation for monthly subs.
  • Steady-state (default, every subsequent run) — scan only the last 7 days. Apply changes incrementally to the saved Subscriptions list. Move entries between Subscriptions and Cancelled subscriptions as state changes. No historical re-scan.

Sources (fetch in parallel)

  • Gmail — receipts, charge alerts, renewal reminders, cancellation confirmations. Window is 90 days on bootstrap, 7 days in steady state.
  • Memory — saved Subscriptions list and Cancelled subscriptions list (with all fields from Section 4 of the lens: payee, what it's for, amount + cadence, started, last charged, total spent, state, cancelled date, cancel reason).
  • Connected integrations — Spotify play count, GitHub activity, Calendar attendance — usage signals for cancel-candidate detection. Skip silently if not connected.

Processing

  1. Cluster this run's money emails by payee + amount (± 5% to absorb FX/tax drift).
  2. For each cluster, derive: payee, what it's for (from receipt body or product name; see Section 4 of lens), amount, cadence, occurrences.
  3. State transitions:
    • New payee, 1st or 2nd occurrence → watching. Not surfaced.
    • 3rd occurrence → promote to active. Add to Subscriptions list. Surface under "New this week."
    • Active, expected charge arrived → update last charged + total spent. Stay in Subscriptions.
    • Active, expected charge missing past grace window (cadence + 7 days) → possible cancellation. Stay in Subscriptions, flagged.
    • Possible cancellation, second missed window OR cancellation email seen → cancelled. Move from Subscriptions to Cancelled subscriptions. Set cancelled date and capture cancel reason if known.
    • Active, lens cancel-candidate rules match (low usage, high cost, I tagged it) → mark cancel candidate. Stay in Subscriptions, flagged.
    • Trial renewal email seen → mark trial about to convert with conversion date.
  4. Save both lists back to memory under stable keys (e.g. subscriptions.active, subscriptions.cancelled) so the Weekly Money Brief and chat queries can read them.

Output

Append to today's scratchpad under <h3>Subscriptions — Sunday Month Day</h3>. Post a Slack message with just the deltas (counts and changes), not the full tables.

The scratchpad section always renders both the Subscriptions and Cancelled subscriptions tables, even if no change this week, so the page is a complete ledger I can scan.

Subscriptions

Full active list. Sorted by monthly cost descending. Three columns:

  • Amount — e.g. $9.99/month, $120/year (cadence inline)
  • Since — started date (e.g. "Mar 2024")
  • What it's for — short description (e.g. "Music streaming", "AI code editor", "Lenny's Newsletter")

Annotate rows that are cancel candidates with a flag and the why. Annotate possible-cancellation rows. Annotate trial-converting rows with the conversion date.

Cancelled subscriptions

Full cancelled list. Sorted by cancelled date descending. Three columns:

  • Amount — what it was costing when cancelled, with cadence
  • Cancelled on — confirmed cancellation date
  • What it was for — short description

If the cancel reason is known, include it in a fourth column or inline.

This week

Below the two tables, a short summary of what changed this run. Skip subsections that are empty:

  • New — subscriptions promoted to active this week (payee + what it's for)
  • Moved to cancelled — subscriptions that transitioned to cancelled this week
  • New cancel candidates — newly flagged with the why (e.g. $15/mo, no Spotify plays in 67 days)
  • Possible cancellations — missed renewal, not yet confirmed
  • Trial conversions imminent — trials renewing in the next 7 days

Rules

  • Never re-scan history if memory already has the subscription state — incremental only.
  • Cancelled subscriptions are never deleted from memory — they stay in the Cancelled subscriptions section as a permanent record.
  • Never recommend a cancel without naming the reason.
  • If I correct "what it's for" on a row in chat, persist the correction to memory so future runs use the corrected description.
  • The Weekly Money Brief reads subscriptions.active for its renewal-watch section — keep the memory key stable.
  • Always append to the scratchpad, never replace. Each week's scratchpad entry is a snapshot of the ledger at that time.

money_weekly_brief.title: Weekly Money Brief
money_weekly_brief.description:
Goal: Every Monday morning, deliver a short weekly brief covering last week's big charges, the renewal watch for the coming week, weekly category totals, and anything needing manual review. Lets me start the week with the money picture in one place.

Applies the lens: "How I Categorize My Money" — Section 1 (email types), Section 3 (payee mapping), Section 6 (what's notable), plus the subscription list saved by the Weekly Subscription Identifier.

Sources (fetch in parallel)

  • Gmail — last 7 days of charge alerts, receipts, and refund emails; next 7 days of renewal reminders.
  • Memory — last week's subscription list (from the Weekly Subscription Identifier task), prior 4-week category averages.

Processing

  1. Big charges — apply Section 6 thresholds per category. Anything over the threshold is notable. Also surface: first-seen payees, duplicate charges within 24h from the same payee, foreign-currency charges if not travelling.
  2. Renewal watch — read renewal reminder emails for the next 7 days. Cross-reference each with the active subscription list. Annotate any that are flagged cancel candidates per the lens.
  3. Weekly category totals — sum categorized spend by category group (Fixed obligations, Recurring services, Daily life, etc.). Compare each to its 4-week trailing average. Flag any category > 25% above average.
  4. Needs my review — uncategorized charges from the week, ambiguous splits (Amazon / Costco with no line items), unknown new payees.

Output

Post a Slack message and append to today's scratchpad under <h3>Money — Week of Month Day</h3>. Four sections:

  • Big charges — table with date, payee, amount, category, why it's notable. Cap at 7 entries.
  • Renewals next 7 days — table with date, payee, amount, cancel-candidate flag if applicable. Cap at 10.
  • Category totals — bullets, only categories that ran or that crossed the 25% trigger. Format: {category} — total (delta vs avg).
  • Needs my review — bullets with each item and what's ambiguous. Cap at 5.

Rules

  • Always include a link or message-id reference per surfaced charge so I can verify against the source email.
  • Skip empty sections. Don't pad.
  • If all four sections are empty, send a one-line quiet-week note and stop.
  • Always append to the scratchpad, never replace.

money_true_expense_forecast.title: Monthly True Expense Forecast
money_true_expense_forecast.description:
Goal: On the 1st of every month, refresh the True Expense list stored in memory using only the past month's data, and surface what's landing this month with the monthly setaside required to absorb the list without surprise.

Applies the lens: "How I Categorize My Money" — Section 5 (True Expenses), Section 3 (payee mapping).

Two run modes

  • Bootstrap (first run, or memory has no True Expense state) — scan the last 12 months of Gmail to identify all True Expenses. A full year is needed to catch annual cadences with ≥2 occurrences.
  • Steady-state (default, every other run) — scan only the last 30 days. Apply changes incrementally to the saved list. No historical re-scan.

Sources (fetch in parallel)

  • Gmail — receipts and charge alerts. Window is 12 months on bootstrap, 30 days in steady state.
  • Memory — saved True Expense list (payee, cadence, expected amount, next expected date, monthly slice, occurrence history), last month's forecast for diffing.

Steady-state processing (incremental against saved state)

  1. Existing True Expense, expected charge arrived this month → update last-seen, refresh next-expected date, recompute monthly slice if amount changed.
  2. Existing True Expense, expected charge missing past its window → flag as possibly cancelled; do not remove until confirmed.
  3. New large non-monthly charge this month from a payee not in the list → check Section 5 of the lens. If it qualifies and the payee has ≥2 occurrences across history, promote to confirmed True Expense. If only one occurrence, add as low-confidence and surface for review.
  4. Forecast computation — filter the maintained list to entries whose next expected date falls in the current month.
  5. Setaside computation — sum monthly slices across the full True Expense list.
  6. Diff against last month's forecast — anything new, anything that moved (price up, cadence changed), anything flagged for possible cancellation.
  7. Save the updated True Expense list back to memory.

Output

Append to today's scratchpad under <h3>True Expense Forecast — Month Year</h3> and post a Slack message with the highlights. Sections (skip any that are empty):

  • Due this month — table with payee, expected date, expected amount, last year's amount for comparison, confidence flag.
  • Monthly setaside — one line stating the total monthly setaside across all True Expenses.
  • Changes vs last forecast — bullets for new / moved / possibly-cancelled True Expenses.
  • Low-confidence forecasts — items with fewer than two historical occurrences that should be confirmed manually.

Rules

  • Never re-scan 12 months if memory already has the True Expense state — incremental only.
  • Do not auto-add a True Expense without at least two occurrences in history — surface as low-confidence instead.
  • If the same payee appears as both a subscription (monthly) and a True Expense (annual upgrade), keep them separate.
  • Always append to the scratchpad, never replace.

email_management.title: Email Management
email_management.description:
Goal: Every 3 hours, look for emails (both sent and received) that came in the last 1 hour, then apply labels using the Email Triage and Labelling skill.

Note: If no emails arrived in the last 1 hour, no action is required — stop the task.

While applying labels, also gather all email thread IDs from the last 1 hour search so that actions can be taken on those emails later.

Once a label is applied, take the following action based on the label

  • Trying To Sell: Draft a polite decline response in the same email thread.
  • Needs Reply: Auto-draft a response using the manik email writing style skill.
  • Action Required: Create a subtask in CORE in Todo status, adding the work item details and email metadata so that the agent can search for that email later if needed.
  • Awaiting Reply (sent emails only): Create a reminder after 2 days. Do not create reminders for sent emails with a Trying To Sell label, since those are already being declined.
  • Any other label: No further action beyond applying the label.

Execution order

Task 1: Apply labels. Task 2: Based on the label type, perform the corresponding action above.

Important: Run silently. No Slack updates under any circumstance.
